legal_framework.supreme_court_case: S. Rajaseekaran v. Union of India WP(C) 295/2012

rights_of_good_samaritan: Cannot be detained by police or hospital authorities
Cannot be forced to reveal personal identity or contact details
Cannot be forced to become a witness; testimony is purely voluntary
Cannot be held liable for any civil or criminal action for assisting in good faith
Cannot be made liable for any medical expenses or treatment costs of the victim
Entitled to get a standard acknowledgment from the hospital upon request
Eligible for a cash reward of Rs. 5000 and a certificate of appreciation under MoRTH scheme
Police questioning (if witness option is selected) must be on a single occasion, at convenience (e.g., home or office), in plain clothes

steps_to_help.action: Ensure scene safety — check for fire, gas leaks, oncoming traffic, and turn on vehicle hazard lights

steps_to_help.note: Golden Hour — first 60 minutes after trauma is critical to prevent permanent damage or death

steps_to_help.action: Check responsiveness and breathing — tap shoulders and ask if they are okay, check chest rise
Control visible bleeding — apply direct pressure using a clean cloth or bandage
Keep the victim stable and comfortable — do not move them unless there is immediate danger
Assist transport or wait for help — accompany to the nearest Level 1/2 Trauma Center if ambulance is delayed

what_NOT_to_do: Do NOT offer water, food, or oral medicine to an unconscious or semi-conscious victim (severe choking risk)
Do NOT move the victim's neck or spine unless absolutely necessary to avoid causing permanent paralysis
Do NOT attempt to remove a helmet from an accident victim if head or neck trauma is suspected
Do NOT crowd around the victim; ensure they have ample ventilation and space to breathe
Do NOT leave the victim unattended once you have committed to assisting them

first_aid_basics.instructions: If the victim is unresponsive and not breathing, place hands in the center of the chest and push hard and fast (100-120 compressions per minute). Provide rescue breaths if trained.
Place a clean cloth over the wound and apply firm, direct, continuous pressure with both hands. Do not remove the cloth if it gets soaked; add more layers on top.
Keep the head, neck, and spine aligned. Encourage the victim to remain completely still. Do not let them turn their head.
Lay the victim flat on their back, loosen tight clothing, keep them warm with a blanket, and if possible, elevate their legs by 12 inches (only if no fracture is suspected).

faq.q: Can police arrest me or detain me for helping an accident victim?
faq.a: No. Section 134A explicitly prohibits police from detaining, harassing, or prosecuting Good Samaritans.

faq.q: Do I have to pay the hospital charges for treatment of the victim I brought in?
faq.a: No. Under Supreme Court guidelines, no hospital can force a Good Samaritan to pay for the victim's emergency treatment or sign as a financial guarantor.

faq.q: Can I leave the hospital immediately after dropping the victim?
faq.a: Yes. A Good Samaritan is free to leave the hospital immediately after delivering the victim. The hospital must give you an acknowledgment receipt.

faq.q: What happens if police or hospital staff harasses me?
faq.a: You can call 1800-11-4000 (National Consumer Helpline) or file a complaint with the state medical council or senior police officers. Disciplinary action will be taken against the offending staff.

faq.q: How does the cash reward scheme work?
faq.a: Under the MoRTH scheme, any Good Samaritan who saves the life of an accident victim by getting them to a hospital during the Golden Hour is rewarded with Rs. 5,000 cash and a Certificate of Appreciation.
